coredata.dc:description: Silicon anodes for lithium-ion batteries offer high theoretical capacity but face practical challenges of capacity fading due to significant volumetric changes during charge-discharge cycles. To reveal the underlying mechanisms, we employ reactive force fields (ReaxFFs) in molecular dynamics simulations to conduct atomic analyses of lithiation and delithiation cycles of silicon particles with three diameters. Our simulations demonstrate a volumetric expansion exceeding 280%, primarily along the ⟨110⟩ direction, with an inward movement of the interface between lithiated and unlithiated regions. We introduce a metric, “geometric defect,” derived from the centroid deviation of neighboring atoms, to evaluate the structural integrity of the silicon anode. Geometric defect state of charge curves show a 5% capacity fade due to silicon loss after the initial cycle. Experimental validation confirms a capacity loss exceeding 40% after the first cycle, attributed to internal defects within silicon particles, aligning well with our simulation results.

head.ce:title: Reactive force-field simulation and experimental validation of cyclic defects in silicon anodes for lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Aerospace Engineering and Applied Mechanics, Tongji University, Shanghai 200092, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Aerospace Engineering and Applied Mechanics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Aerospace Engineering and Applied Mechanics, Tongji University, Shanghai 200092, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silicon anodes for lithium-ion batteries offer high theoretical capacity but face practical challenges of capacity fading due to significant volumetric changes during charge-discharge cycles. To reveal the underlying mechanisms, we employ reactive force fields (ReaxFFs) in molecular dynamics simulations to conduct atomic analyses of lithiation and delithiation cycles of silicon particles with three diameters. Our simulations demonstrate a volumetric expansion exceeding 280%, primarily along the ⟨110⟩ direction, with an inward movement of the interface between lithiated and unlithiated regions. We introduce a metric, “geometric defect,” derived from the centroid deviation of neighboring atoms, to evaluate the structural integrity of the silicon anode. Geometric defect state of charge curves show a 5% capacity fade due to silicon loss after the initial cycle. Experimental validation confirms a capacity loss exceeding 40% after the first cycle, attributed to internal defects within silicon particles, aligning well with our simulation results.

Here, Chen et al. validate ReaxFF simulations and experiments, finding a geometric defect metric that can quantitatively evaluate the structural integrity in molecular dynamics models of silicon particles. By calculating the centroid deviation of neighboring atoms during lithiation-delithiation cycles, they show good agreement between experiment and simulation.

ce:section.ce:para:
There are three categories of negative electrode materials for lithium-ion batteries: intercalation materials, conversion materials, and alloys. 1
,
2
,
3 Among these, alloys emerge as a promising option due to their higher Li storage capacity induced by alloying reactions. 4
,
5 Silicon, as one of these alloys, has garnered attention as a promising anode material for lithium-ion batteries, boasting a theoretical capacity as high as 4,200 mAh/g, which surpasses graphite’s 372 mAh/g by an order of magnitude. However, despite its impressive capacity, silicon presents a significant challenge due to its notable volumetric expansion exceeding 300%. This expansion leads to cracking and pulverization of the silicon anode material, resulting in the loss of connectivity between electrodes and the current collector. Consequently, this degradation undermines battery capacity and significantly impacts cyclic performance, 1
,
6 posing a considerable obstacle to practical implementation. 7

To commence, researchers have identified three primary difficulties that hinder the widespread adoption of silicon anode batteries in practical utility: low electronic conductivity, the generation of a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) at the electrode/electrolyte interface, and degradation induced by large volumetric expansion. 1 These challenges are pivotal in understanding the origin of degradation, necessitating concerted efforts to address them effectively. As the silicon will experience phase changes owing to the intercalate of large amount of lithium, anisotropic expansion due to this crystallography will take place during the lithiation. 8
,
9 The anisotropic expansion during the two-phase lithiation of crystalline silicon (c-silicon) anodes not only leads to stress intensification but also induces tensile hoop stress at the surface, exacerbated by concentration gradients associated with volume expansion. 9
,
10 It is anticipated that non-uniform expansion invariably leads to heightened stress levels, thereby precipitating damage and compromising recyclability. Prior investigations indicate that expansion induced by lithiation predominantly occurs at the phase boundary between silicon and multiple Li-rich silicon alloy phases, 9 where stress concentration and anisotropic expansion manifest. Moreover, cyclic volumetric changes inevitably culminate in mechanical damage and pulverization, irrespective of the varied stress levels determined by differing silicon sizes and phases. 6
,
11
,
12
,
13

In an effort to address such issues, investigations on the distribution and dominance of stress effects are needed. A design for silicon film incorporated with Cu foil was presented by Huang et al. 14 to alleviate the stress effects. The uneven structure of the Cu foil could provide enough space for volume expansion, which benefits the release of the stress that occurs on the interface. Zhang et al. 15 constructed a model quantitatively elucidating the impact of stress evolution on capacity fading induced by cyclic charge-discharge. Additionally, they innovatively introduced the concept of pre-injecting sufficient damage as a training mechanism to enhance the anti-degradation performance of batteries, which is conceptually counterintuitive. In a further investigation, Li et al. 16 proposed a comprehensive chemo-mechanical model to numerically describe the degradation with adequate consideration of mechanical damages and chemical damages, incorporating a thorough analysis of the evolution of stress, plastic strain, and damages during multiple cycles and expounding on the implications of surface damages and stress distribution. Sethuraman et al. 17 quantitatively explicate a coupling relationship between stress and electric potential, validated by in situ measurements. They highlight that stress within the silicon anode can significantly impact capacity and energy dissipation resulting from plastic deformation.

Experimental exploration, employing advanced measurement techniques, ingenious designs, and theoretical consolidation incorporating elegant algorithms or finite element method simulations, has predominantly focused on the macroscale aspects of lithium-ion battery research, particularly the evolution of stress, 16
,
18 SEI layers, 19
,
20 and various phases of lithium-silicon crystal compounds. 1
,
21
,
22 Concurrently, some investigations have also delved into the microscale domain. Given the complexity of silicon anode degradation during lithiation and delithiation, which involves multiscale and multifield coupling, analyses rooted in macroscopic perspectives may lack intuitive insights. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, on the other hand, offer unique advantages in elucidating micromechanisms, given their ability to delve deeply into the behavior of individual atoms. 23 Attention has increasingly focused on the prominence of MD simulations, highlighting a viable and efficient approach. This trend has spurred rapid developments in interatomic potential models, recognized as the pivotal factor governing atomistic properties. 24

Cui et al. 25 introduced a second-nearest-neighbor modified embedded atom method (2NN MEAM) potential for Li-Si alloys, enabling the simulation of crystal structural transitions from ordered to disordered states. Their work showcased the potential’s stability under finite temperatures and its capability to predict material properties effectively. Building upon this MEAM potential, Wang et al. 26 observed a transition in atom-scale plasticity mechanisms during their simulations of plasticity and cracking in lithiated silicon anodes.

While classical force fields depict non-reactive interactions based on atomic positions, their inability to model changes in atomic connectivity renders them inadequate for reactive simulations. 27 Other approaches have been developed for the Li–Si system to allow modeling such events. Density functional theory (DFT) has been widely used to explore atomic-level mechanisms, providing high accuracy for electronic structures and reaction mechanisms despite its high computational cost, limitations to small systems, and short timescales. 28
,
29
,
30
,
31 Machine learning techniques, though reliant on training data quality, allow for efficient large-scale simulations. 32
,
33
,
34 Additionally, density functional tight binding offers a balance between computational efficiency and accuracy, making it suitable for larger systems and longer simulations than full DFT, and has demonstrated its high accuracy in a recent model developed for Li–Si alloys. 35
,
36

To be capable of modeling reactive simulations and large-scale atomic systems at the same time, the development of reactive force fields (ReaxFFs) also became imperative, augmenting classical force fields with terms accounting for atomic connections. This enhancement allows the potential function to describe reactive events through empirically calculated bond order (BO) parameters derived from interatomic distances. 27 ReaxFF achieves versatility by treating elements with the same mathematical framework irrespective of their state or phase, thereby facilitating the simulation of atomic behavior across phases, particularly at interfaces. In essence, the revolutionary development of ReaxFF enhances MD simulations, enabling better representation of complex interatomic dynamics and yielding results with better authority.

Alireza et al. led the analysis of the stress effects on c-silicon nanowires (SiNWs) using a comprehensive ReaxFF developed by Duin et al., 23
,
37 providing valuable insights into the lithiation behavior of SiNWs. With ReaxFF simulations, Fan et al. 38 demonstrated that the mechanical properties of amorphous LixSi alloys, such as Young’s modulus and hardness, decrease with increasing lithium content, elucidating the softening effect of lithiation. In a subsequent study, Fan et al. 39 conducted further ReaxFF simulations, which indicate that compressive stresses develop at the phase boundary and hoop tension occurs near the surface of a Si nanopillar, revealing the atomistic basis for the stress induced by lithiation. Basu et al. 40 used ReaxFFs to investigate the structural transformations and damage accumulation, revealing a ductile-brittle-ductile transition in the amorphous silicon (a-Si) anode during both lithiation and delithiation. Using ReaxFFs, Ding et al. 41 found that tensile stress accelerates lithium diffusion, while compressive stress decelerates it, highlighting the significant role of external stress on lithiation uniformity and structural integrity. Kim et al. 42
,
43 used ReaxFFs to study the phase boundary evolution during initial lithiation and the irreversible structural changes during delithiation, respectively, revealing critical mechanisms underlying the degradation and preservation of silicon anodes. Cao et al. 44 applied ReaxFFs to obtain the anisotropic formation and migration of an atomically sharp phase boundary. The resulting stress fields exhibit the development of hoop tension near the nanopillar surface, highlighting the atomistic underpinning for stress generation in two-phase anisotropic lithiation. In Trochet and Mousseau’s findings, 45 they employed the integration of ReaxFFs and kinetic activation-relaxation technique (kART) to enable a detailed analysis of lithium diffusion mechanisms in silicon-like lithium atom migration pathways and energy barriers, offering insights critical for enhancing lithium-ion battery performance.

Independently, Jung et al. 46 developed a ReaxFF for the Li–Si system, observing SiNW behavior during both lithiation and delithiation, including phenomena such as Li⁺-ion penetration and Si–Si bond breakage. Additionally, Guifo et al. 47 constructed a novel ReaxFF for Li/Si/C, tailored for silicon-carbon composite anode materials, and validated its accuracy. As Pan et al. 48 indicated that current ReaxFFs underestimate the valence angle energy and over-coordination energy penalty for c-Si, they developed the ReaxFF parameters for the Li–Si system, with the interactions of Li–Li, Li–Si, and Si–Si being reperformed based on the first principle calculations. Noting that different ReaxFF parameter sets are used or developed in these references, diverse choices of the parameters exist, as the ReaxFF creators may make different trade-offs due to varying research biases. Optimizations are also continually updated to achieve better authenticity.

Though the ReaxFF simulation is highly dependent on the accuracy of the empirical parameters, its role remains highly esteemed in characterizing microbehavior and exploring mechanisms. 49 Given that degradation significantly contributes to capacity fading, the importance of MD-based research on anode structural integrity has emerged to address existing research gaps. However, the comparison between experimental validation and the real-time MD results is indirect due to the substantial difference in timescales between MD simulation and reality. Specifically, MD simulations, spanning millions of steps, only cover a few nanoseconds, whereas real charge-discharge processes last for hours, posing challenges in correlating them. To bridge this gap, a common approach is using the state of charge (SOC) as an intermediary term to represent the reaction process, as the states with the same lithiated amounts of silicon are considered to be making equivalent progress. This approach enables a direct comparison between simulations and experiments, circumventing the disparity in timescales. In this study, we conducted an assessment of the structural integrity of a lithiated crystal silicon sphere during cyclic charge-discharge using ReaxFFs, complemented by experimental validation. We implemented a modified delithiation procedure based on the work by Jung et al. 46 to simulate cyclic charge-discharge with negative electrodes of varying diameters. Given that Li⁺-ion penetration and extraction induce silicon incoherence, we introduced a geometric defect metric based on the centroid shifting of nearest-neighbor atoms to evaluate anode integrity. This facilitated a comprehensive analysis of microbehaviors and their consequential effects. By leveraging a positive correlation between defects and capacity fading, we compared the simulated evolution of defects with cycle number and experimental results to validate the accuracy of the simulation outcomes.

We conducted cycles for spherical silicon anodes with diameters of 54, 81, and 108 Å using LAMMPS, 50 an MD simulator for large atomic systems. To visualize and analyze every frame of output, we utilized OVITO software, known for its capabilities in visualization post-processing. 51 We also explain the concept of geometric defect based on the centroid deviation of neighboring silicon atoms within a cutoff of 3 Å.
The ReaxFF simulation, the concept of geometric defect, and the experimental preparation are presented in greater detail in the experimental procedures.

In this subsection, we conducted a comprehensive analysis of various mechanical properties derived from observations of atomic behavior. We present several cross-sectional diagrams of the silicon sphere during lithiation as the SOC increases in Figure 1 A. Initially, in the presence of a highly concentrated lithium solution, lithium atoms infiltrate the anode primarily at its periphery. As diffusion progresses, the reaction front propagates inward, resulting in a sparse arrangement of silicon atoms in the lithiated region due to the widening atomic spacing, while the unlithiated core maintains a dense crystal configuration with high regularity. Such a core-shell atomic structure was also observed by previous studies. 39
,
46 Eventually, the crystal core diminishes until it dissipates completely, leading to a highly disordered arrangement of silicon atoms once the negative electrode is fully lithiated.

As
Li 3.75
Si
is regarded as the fully lithiated state in this paper, which is referred to and accepted by previous research, 46
,
52 the SOC reached a maximum of 99.6% in our simulation, indicating an ultimate state of
Li 3.74
Si
.

An illustration of atomic redistribution during a segment of delithiation is also depicted in Figures 1B and 1C. These figures show the atomic redistribution during a segment of delithiation. Parts of the silicon situated at the boundary are exposed, while lithium is spatially confined within a sphere due to the final deletion of the lithium shell. During relaxation, lithium gradually emerges and reoccupies the surface due to the concentration gradient, causing the exposed silicon to shrink rapidly. Additionally, some scattered silicon atoms are observed wandering across the simulation box, likely due to impact-induced segregation caused by the outward movement of lithium atoms.

Then, we examined the volumetric expansion and shrinkage during the cycle. Considering that
Li 3.75
Si
represents the ultimate state of a Li–Si compound after full lithiation, we calculated the volumetric change rate using the following formula 52 :
(Equation 1)

While the initial silicon anode maintains a highly regular spherical shape, a lithiated silicon anode deviates from its original form and adopts a distorted configuration with prominent protrusions at the eight corners of a cubic box. This anisotropic volumetric expansion demonstrates a faster expansion rate along the⟨110⟩orientation compared to other orientations, driven by a faster lithium diffusive velocity in this direction, as illustrated in Figures 2A and 2B. This finding is consistent with previous research by Lee et al. and Jung et al. 46
,
53 Calculating the silicon volume based on the assumption of a uniform sphere would result in computational errors. Therefore, we utilized the alpha shape function in the computational module of MATLAB, which constructs a 3D object tightly enclosing discrete points within its edges without internal pores (Figure 2C).

Taking the silicon sphere with a diameter of 108 Å as our research object, we find that the volumetric expansion rate reaches as high as 280% under a fully lithiated state, which closely approximates the theoretical prediction by Xu at 269%. 52 Meanwhile, the upper and lower limits of results computed as a sphere are 378% and 210%, respectively. We also constructed a relationship between the volumetric expansion rate and the SOC, as shown in Figure 2D. Ignoring the influence generated by the fragmentation treatment of delithiation, we hypothesized that the volume change during delithiation is consistent. Therefore, a roughly linear relationship is revealed in the illustration. As the lithiation degree quantitatively determines the amount of transformed silicon atoms, lithiated silicon possesses a different atomic configuration that quantitatively occupies a certain space, leading to a direct relationship between the volumetric expansion and the microstructure changes, which aligns well with our expectations. In the fully lithiated state, the silicon sphere is considered to have an equivalent diameter of 169.5 Å. Additionally, we observed a volume deficit of over 5%, as indicated by the shaded area in Figure 4D. This may be attributed to the detachment of silicon damaged during the cycle. During operation, the heterogeneous over-280% volume expansion that occurs during charge-discharge cycles can induce mechanical stress, resulting in particle cracking and compromising the structural integrity of the anode. This stress, along with SEI layer degradation, reduces the reversible capacity of the silicon anode, causing rapid capacity fade and impacting long-term performance. Additionally, uneven expansion and contraction affect cycle stability, further degrading performance over time. Addressing these issues is critical for improving the performance and longevity of silicon-based lithium-ion batteries.

We conducted an investigation into the relationship between the position of the reaction front and the lithiation process. In our analysis, we observed a gradual increase in the first peak to around 2.8 in the radial distribution function analysis for lithiating silicon anodes, indicating a phase change during lithiation. 23 This change results in the formation of an interface between the lithiated and unlithiated areas. 43 Considering
Li 3.75
Si
as the ultimately lithiated state, the average bond length for Si–Li is 2.71 Å, with each silicon atom surrounded by 12 lithium atoms in such a configuration. 52 Furthermore, any lithium atom within a spherical region centered on the silicon atom is considered to be bonded, as the ReaxFF is a distance-dependent potential. Based on this, we introduce an index of Si–Li bond numbers to estimate the coordination state by counting nearby lithium atoms with a cutoff at 2.7 Å.

Figure 3 A illustrates a cross-section under a specific SOC in the coordination analysis. The coordination distribution exhibits complete radial symmetry. Si–Li bonds connected to a single silicon atom can range from 0 to 12, with colors changing from red to blue as the depth increases, reflecting an inward reaction trend. An evident core-shell structure is observed, where the outer shell transitions to an amorphous state due to high lithiation, while the dark core in the middle maintains a crystalline configuration. This structure distinctly delineates the interface between crystal silicon and the amorphous Li–Si compound. To better visualize the movement of the interface, Figure 3B employs two distinct colors to represent silicon atoms in different states: yellow for lithiated silicon and purple for unlithiated silicon. Initially, no amorphous Li–Si compound is present, but as the reaction initiates, an a-Si shell rapidly forms. The dark crystal core resides within this interface, gradually diminishing in size as the interface is pushed inward with increasing SOC, corresponding to the movement of the reaction front. If the SOC exceeds 90%, no visible crystal core remains, and the interface vanishes along with the core.

Given the high spherical symmetry exhibited by the atomic behaviors of silicon and lithium, we conducted an investigation into the numerical distribution of Si–Li bonds along the radial axis during lithiation. As the dimensions of the silicon sphere continually change, accurately describing locations relative to the radius using absolute coordinates becomes challenging. Therefore, we employed an equivalent depth to indicate the radial position of atoms or a phase interface. The equivalent depth is defined as the ratio of absolute depth to the equivalent radius under the current SOC. To facilitate this, we extracted a thin strip extending from the centroid of the sphere to the outer edge, resulting in a cuboid filled with silicon atoms. This cuboid has a length equal to the equivalent radius and a lateral area of 10 × 10 Å. We divided the equivalent radius into 20 evenly spaced segments and calculated the average bond number of all atoms within each segment separately. These data were then used to plot the bond-depth diagram, as shown in Figure 4 A.

Figure 4A illustrates the distribution of Si–Li coordination along the radial axis at SOC = 70%. The lower strip presents the radial atomic arrangement, color coded based on a spectrum bar, corresponding to the upper curve. The curve remains at 0 within the crystal core range, indicating the unlithiated inner region. As the equivalent depth gradually decreases, a sharp increase occurs, marking the interface between c-silicon (c-Si) and a-Si. This abrupt change signifies the interface’s position. The curve maintains relatively high values, as the outer silicon has undergone significant lithiation. Figure 4B depicts a curve illustrating the radial movement of the interface with SOC. The interface progresses inward as lithiation advances, consistent with previous findings. Notably, the rate of depth increase is lower at the beginning of lithiation compared to the end. This discrepancy arises from the spherical geometric property, where the outer shell occupies more silicon atoms than the inner shell despite the SOC being calculated based on the lithiated amount. In contrast, if the independent variable changes to time, then the velocity of the interface will be faster at the beginning due to a much faster lithiation rate at the start, which aligns with the trend presented by Ding et al. 41 Although there is an absence of applied electric fields within the diffusive Li–Si system in our simulation, the charge transfer still exists due to the reaction; further details of the analysis for charge transfer are presented in Note S1 and Figure S1.

In this subsection, we conducted a defect analysis to assess the integrity of the silicon sphere. The investigation encompassed anodes with diameters of 54, 81, and 108 Å throughout the entire cycle. Here, we present a heatmap illustrating the evolution of defects during lithiation, as depicted in Figure 5 A, with the cross-section extracted from the 108 Å diameter anode.

As stated in the experimental procedures, defects are identified when the centroid deviation exceeds 1 Å within a range of 0 to 3 Å. It is important to note that initial defects may be visually apparent due to brighter colors at the boundary. This occurs because the cutoff-dependent algorithm assigns non-zero values to outermost atoms lacking outside neighbors. Such initial defects are acceptable, as surface imperfections are inherent in natural entities. Therefore, we consider these defects as indicative of initial surface roughness.

The core-shaped continuum maintains high integrity, represented by dark blue areas indicating low centroid deviation values, while the outer shell exhibits a spatial distribution of defects colored by the spectrum. Hues closer to red indicate more pronounced geometric distortion. As the SOC increases, there is a noticeable infiltration of defects toward the inside of the sphere, accompanied by the collapse of the crystal structure. In Figures 5B–5D, we present separate defect-SOC curve diagrams for anode diameters of 54, 81, and 108 Å. These curves depict the proportion of all defects on the anode, referred to as general geometric defects, on the vertical axis. This dimensionless term serves as a measure for integrity evaluation. A higher defect percentage correlates with lower integrity. All three diagrams exhibit the same trend in the curve. By observing the intersection of the y axis and the red line representing lithiation, we can determine the initial defects directly. As the diameter increases, a size effect becomes evident as the initial defects gradually decrease due to the smaller surface-to-volume ratio. The curve demonstrates a positive correlation between defects and SOC, with defects typically exceeding 35%. In the blue curve representing delithiation, we observe an initial rise in defects, indicating the structural impact of lithium atoms’ outward movement. This is attributed to the peeling of damaged silicon fragments during lithiation, leading to the generation of discrete wandering silicon clusters as depicted in Figures 1B and 1C. Subsequent delithiation leads to a decline in the curve, although it does not coincide with the original point by the end, resulting in a gap of over 4% on the y axis. We hypothesize that this gap represents the accumulated irreversible defects throughout the entire cycle, which likely contribute to the deterioration of the silicon electrode.

Repeating the lithiation and delithiation, we further investigated the defect-SOC relationship based on the 108-Å-diameter silicon sphere as displayed in Figure 6 . The second cycle closely follows the basic trend of the first one. The enlarged area reveals that the total integrity loss induced by the second cycle is much smaller than the first, at less than 1%. This disparity arises from the initial lithiation being established on the crystal structure with a regular configuration, while the second lithiation does not. The initial lithiation requires 300 timesteps to reach a SOC of 99.6%, whereas the second lithiation only reaches a SOC of 95.2% in the same amount of time under the same lithium concentration. Even with an additional 100 timesteps allowed for the reaction to proceed, achieving the fully lithiated state remains challenging. Hence, we infer that the lost 5% can be attributed to the contribution of capacity fading induced by the loss of active materials of silicon anodes. This loss of 5% approximately matches the volume loss depicted in Figure 2 and the accumulated damage shown in Figure 5, indicating a causal relationship between structural deterioration and functional degradation.
The same relationship can also be demonstrated by Figure 7 , which presents several semi-combined diagrams capable of visualizing the results of both defect and coordination analyses simultaneously.

It is evident from the annular separatrix that the redistribution of defects aligns with the movement of the SOC. Based on our observations and analysis, we can deduce the following sequence of events: (1) the reaction front moves radially inward, (2) the lithiated silicon undergoes a phase change, and (3) this phase change results in the rearrangement of silicon, accompanied by volumetric expansion and the formation of interfaces and defects. So, the inward movement of the interface refers to the increase of the geometric defect and actually indicates the decline of the structural integrity during lithiation, which would harm the performance and durability of the silicon anodes.

To facilitate a comparison between the experimental and simulation results, we adopt the following methods to process data. Firstly, the initial lithiation capacity was used to normalize the lithiation/delithiation capacity of the experiment, yielding the experimental cycling capacity retention. Secondly, assuming a positive correlation between the capacity decrease and the geometry defects of the silicon particle, the percentage of defects represents the percentage of capacity decrease. Thus, the relation between the lithiation capacity retention of the k th cycle,
Q k
l i t h i a t i o n
, and the defect after the k th lithiation process,
D k
l i t h i a t i o n
, is obtained,
(Equation 2)

= 1 −

Q k

, is determined by

is the defect after the k th delithiation process. The simulated capacity retention results were thereby obtained.

Figure 8 compares experimental and simulation data, with solid lines representing the simulation results and dashed lines representing the experimental results. The dashed line is obtained by calculating the average value of several repeated experiments, and the margin of error is determined by calculating the standard deviation. Considering the limitation of computational resources and the adequacy of certain numbers of simulations for the analysis, 3 cycles are preferred. Initially, with unlithiated silicon in the 0th cycle, only lithiation occurs. Thus, both experimental and theoretical studies begin with the lithiation of silicon, making the 0th cycle solely about lithiation capacity. For simplicity, the delithiation capacity in the 0th cycle is considered zero, leading to an apparent reverse trend in the 0–1 cycle. As it is found that the two results match well, the comparison suggests the accuracy of the modeling method as well as the proposed concept of geometric defects, and it is noteworthy that there is approximately a 40% capacity drop caused by damage generated from the internal defects of silicon particles following the initial lithiation-delithiation cycle, which is different from the capacity loss induced by the loss of silicon. This drop can be attributed to the evolution of defects during the phase transition of silicon from the crystalline state to the amorphous state, as indicated by the simulation results provided above. Subsequently, the capacity fading is relatively gradual. The capacity retention in the simulation results is slightly higher than that of the experimental data. This difference can be attributed to the omission of side reactions involving the formation of SEI layers in the simulations. These reactions can consume lithium and contribute to capacity fading.

This paper presents MD simulations encompassing both lithiation and delithiation of silicon-based anodes with varying dimensions using ReaxFF. Observations focused on atomic behavior verify an anisotropic volumetric expansion, primarily along the⟨110⟩orientation, reaching 280% during lithiation, followed by a 5% volume loss during subsequent delithiation due to silicon disengagement. Additionally, we developed a coordination analysis based on the bond number attached to a single silicon atom to investigate the movement of the interface between c-Si in the unlithiated region and a-Si in the lithiated region. The radial inward movement of the interface is depicted by bond-SOC-related contours and curves with radial distributions of Li–Si coordination. Furthermore, a defect analysis based on the centroid deviation of nearest-neighbor silicon atoms is employed to inspect the structural integrity. By implementing this criterion, we discuss the distribution and influence of newly defined defects during cycles. Results indicate that defects redistribute synchronously with the movement of the interface, and the initial cycle leads to a capacity fading of 5% induced by silicon loss, highly correlated with volume loss and accumulated defects during the first cycle, revealing a causal pathway from structural deterioration to functional degradation. Our experimental validation investigates capacity retention over three cycles and identifies over 40% capacity fade induced by geometric defects. Comparison curves demonstrate good agreement between simulations and experiments. Overall, this study clarifies the nanoscale underlying mechanism of capacity loss for silicon-alloy lithium-ion batteries caused by the cyclic defects during lithiation-delithiation cycles.

Since the issues with silicon anodes have been continually studied, several approaches to mitigate the impact of volumetric expansion have been proposed. Nanoscale structural designs, such as nanowires and porous structures, have been introduced to better accommodate the expansion and maintain structural integrity. Silicon composite materials, such as carbon/silicon composites and metal silicides, have been developed to improve the stability and performance of silicon anodes. As optimizations are rapidly updated, it is hoped that this work on the damage of mechanism of lithiated silicon can provide greater understanding of and insight into addressing these issues.
Further information and requests for resources and reagents should be directed to and will be fulfilled by the lead contact, Ning Liu (21019@tongji.edu.cn).
This study did not generate new unique reagents.

The LAMMPS code used for reproducibility has been included in the supplemental information (Note S1). Any additional information required to reanalyze the data reported in this paper is available from the lead contact upon request.

To facilitate ReaxFF simulations, we utilized potentials independently developed by Jung et al., 46 which incorporated Li and Si parameter sets trained by Duin et al. 54 and Han et al. 55 Employing the open-source software LAMMPS 50 with the additional ReaxFF package, we conducted simulations in an NVT ensemble at 300 K. The timestep was set to 1 fs, while the temperature damping coefficient and pressure damping coefficient were sequentially set at 300 and 100
f s
− 1
, respectively. In addition to their ease of preparation for experiments, spherical silicon anodes offer several advantages, including shorter lithium-ion diffusion distances, improved structural integrity, and the ability to accommodate the volume expansion of silicon. As a result, spherical silicon particles were chosen for use in both simulations and experiments. We constructed three spherical silicon-anode models with diameters of 54, 81, and 108 Å, each surrounded by an intermediate buffer zone with a thickness of 5 Å within an encapsulation of high-concentration lithium solution during lithiation. As the spontaneous diffusion of the Li–Si system occupies great time and computational resources, such a high concentration of lithium is needed to obtain a faster diffusion rate and reasonably reduce the research costs, which also aligns with reality since the realistic lithium disc provides tremendous Li. The concentration for lithium solution in MD simulation is about
9.7 ×
10 4
mol /
m 3
. During experiments, lithium ions undergo electrochemical reactions on the surface of silicon electrode particles, reducing to lithium atoms. Due to the relatively low diffusion rate of lithium in silicon particles, lithium tends to accumulate on the particle surface, reaching concentrations on the order of
10 5
mol /
m 3
, 56
,
57 which is consistent with the values used in MD simulations. Each model underwent 500,000 steps to ensure thorough lithiation of the silicon anode, facilitating subsequent delithiation processes.

Regarding delithiation, we implemented a different gradient of lithium removal compared to Jung’s approach, 46 aimed at optimizing the delithiation rate. Presuming that outermost lithium atoms detach first from silicon’s grasp, manual operations were preferred, starting from the edge of the sphere due to technical constraints, rather than relying on a voltage-dependent force field in a model setting, which could automatically drive lithium extraction against concentration differences. In Jung’s method, 46 delithiation involved sequentially repeating the cyclic treatment of removing a specific amount of lithium atoms within the lithiated surface by a fixed thickness, followed by relaxation for a certain period. However, thickness-dependent removal may lead to irregular decreases due to geometric variations, where shells of different external diameters contain different quantities of atoms. Therefore, we recommend omitting lithium atoms based on a fixed SOC decrease rather than a fixed thickness, wherein the SOC is calculated as the ratio of the number of lithium atoms actually bonded to silicon to the maximum number of lithium atoms that can theoretically bond to silicon in our simulation. Besides achieving a smoother delithiation procedure, the SOC-dependent removal pattern facilitates experimental validation more conveniently. We set the SOC decrease at 10%, indicating ten repeated operations for a single delithiation procedure. Subsequently, in MATLAB, we processed the dump file obtained from the last step of the previous procedure, removing lithium atoms outside a manually determined spherical region to maintain the SOC at a preset value. The remaining lithium atoms were retained, and a new data file was created for the subsequent relaxation. Each relaxation step in every lithiation-delithiation cycle followed the same procedure as per control principles. Such delithiation, controlled by manually deleting lithium atoms, ignores the stage of outward lithium movement. This process, combined with the faster diffusion induced by high concentration, accelerates the entire cycle and makes the simulation time saving.

Throughout the lithiation and delithiation phases, silicon atoms endure persistent squeezing or dragging due to lithium insertion or extraction. This process initiates the breaking of Si–Si bonds and leads to the collapse of the crystal silicon arrangement, starting from the lithiated region and resulting in a breakdown of structural integrity. To assess the basic integrity conditions of the silicon sphere, we introduced a defect-evaluation criterion. The criterion involves determining the estimated silicon atom (a process executed for every atom) and grouping the nearest-neighbor silicon atoms. Numerically, it is based on the centroid deviation of the nearest-neighbor atoms' group from the evaluated atom’s original position. To define the nearest-neighbor group, we first consider the crystal silicon structure to provide a reference for drafting the criterion. To accomplish this, we imported a complete atomic model of the silicon sphere into OVITO, and coordination analysis revealed the radial distribution of the unlithiated silicon structure, as depicted in Figure 9 .

The first peak observed in the pair separation distance is situated at 2.35 Å, supporting the Si–Si bond length, consistent with the findings of Ostadhossein et al. and Jung et al. 23
,
46 When implementing the criterion, our developed program creates a spherical region centered on the estimated atom’s position, with a radius determined by the cutoff to filter out atoms outside the neighborhood. Notably, we observed that the peak value oscillates within a narrow range between 2.3 and 2.8 Å during the lithiation or delithiation process. Therefore, we set a cutoff radius at 3 Å as a reasonable threshold for defining participating atoms. Subsequently, we calculated the centroid of the neighbor group and determined the distance between the centroid and the estimated atom. The centroid formula is provided again below:
(Equation 6)

The crystal silicon adheres to the diamond spatial configuration, resulting in a structurally symmetrical environment for a single silicon atom within a spherical region, initially yielding a centroid deviation of 0 for the internal atoms. However, lithium’s dynamic movement disrupts this symmetry by stretching or breaking the Si–Si bonds, where the gradual chaos was proved to be destined by Basu et al., 40 as they obtained the structural signature of accumulation of atomic level disorder during a ReaxFF simulation. The onset of a defect leads to disorder in the surrounding configuration, resulting in a rapid numerical shift in the centroid deviation and subsequent integrity decline. To make this concept better understood, a demonstrative diagram shown in Figure 10 describes a geometric microdistortion due to the insertion of lithium atoms in the silicon film, wherein the orange and blue atoms represent the silicon atoms and lithium atoms, respectively. The defect analysis has been conducted on the silicon atoms, as the right column shows. Owing to the imbalanced surroundings, the values of the silicon near the insertion will be much higher than other atoms with uniform surroundings, indicating the decline of the local structural integrity. To assess the atom’s condition, we establish a threshold. If the calculated centroid deviation exceeds this threshold, then we classify the atom as broken, and the proportion of broken silicon is defined as a geometric-defect-related term. In line with Li’s findings, 58 we set the threshold at 1.0.

Silicon-based CR2032 coin half-cells were prepared. For the working electrodes, the pre-coated electrode sheets with a thickness of ∼25 μm were procured from Hangzhou EverStarry Technologies. The slurry comprises silicon powder with a diameter of ∼25 nm, Super P as the conductive agent, and polyacrylic acid as the binder. The silicon-based working electrodes with 12 mm radius were cut from the pre-coated sheets. Utilizing lithium disks (China Energy Lithium) as the counter electrode, along with a commercial electrolyte (from Tinci Materials Technology) consisting of 1.0 M LiPF6 in a volume ratio of EC:DEC = 3:7, supplemented with 10 vol % FEC, and circular separators (2325, Celgard), the coin half-cells were assembled within a glove box containing an argon atmosphere, ensuring that water vapor and oxygen levels remained below 0.1 ppm. The total mass of the half-cell is 3.2753 g, comprising a 1.765 g battery casing, 0.24 g spring, 1.2 g spacer, 0.02 g separator, 0.04 g lithium disk, and 0.0103 g silicon electrode. The areal mass of the electrode is 0.708 mg/cm2.

Electrochemical cycling tests were conducted by a battery testing system (CT-3008T, Neware) in a temperature chamber (MHW-200, Neware) at a constant temperature of 25°C. To mimic the simulation condition, the potentiostatic operation was applied with the voltage window from 0.01 to 1 V. To mitigate the initial large current during delithiation, a cutoff current of 2 mA was implemented. It is important to note that no formation cycles were conducted prior to the cycling tests, ensuring that the silicon particles were in a crystalline state at the outset of the cycling tests.
